nativeRange.habitat: The Cardinal Tetra is native to forest streams and slow-moving minor tributaries within igapó and igarapé flooded forest ecosystems — the blackwater river systems of the Amazon and Orinoco basins. These waters are tannin-stained, highly acidic, and extremely soft, filtering down through decaying leaf litter before the fish encounter them.

diet.notes: Micropredator omnivore that tends to accept most small prepared foods readily, including flakes, nano/micropellets, freeze-dried tubifex, and frozen foods such as baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, and bloodworm. Live foods are generally relished.

summary: The Cardinal Tetra is an iconic Amazonian schooling fish prized for its vivid, full-length red belly and brilliant iridescent blue stripe. Generally considered intermediate in difficulty, it is sensitive to poor water quality and prefers soft, acidic conditions.
careNotes: Cardinal Tetras tend to do best in mature, well-filtered aquariums with stable water chemistry — sudden parameter swings are generally more harmful than absolute values. Wild-caught specimens are significantly more demanding than captive-bred individuals and often require a pH well below 6.0 with very soft water to acclimate properly. Captive-bred stock is considerably hardier and more tolerant of slightly harder, more neutral water (up to around pH 7.0 and 10 dGH). A school of at least 8 is advisable for natural schooling behavior and reduced stress; 10–15 is preferable in larger setups. The species is commonly confused with the Neon Tetra (Paracheirodon innesi), which is smaller and has red colouration only on the rear half of the body — the Cardinal Tetra's red extends the full body length. A note on taxonomy: a 2024 phylogenomic study reclassified this species into the family Acestrorhamphidae rather than the traditional Characidae, though most aquarium hobby sources continue to list Characidae.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is considered advanced and is rarely attempted outside of dedicated setups. A separate species tank is generally needed, conditioned to very soft water (near-zero hardness), acidic conditions (pH 5.0–6.0), dim or blackout lighting, and a slight temperature reduction of a few degrees to encourage spawning. Spawning tends to occur nocturnally, often around midnight. Eggs are highly light-sensitive and typically hatch within 24–36 hours; fry become free-swimming within 3–4 days. Raising the fry is widely considered the most difficult stage, as they require infusoria and very fine live or prepared foods in their earliest days. The great majority of Cardinal Tetras sold in the aquarium trade are wild-caught from the Rio Negro rather than captive-bred.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, water parameters, native range, tank dimensions, and breeding notes. Hardness given as 18–215 ppm (≈1–12 dGH); lower end recommended for wild/breeding stock. pH 3.5–7.5 with lower end preferred.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 3.0 cm SL, temperature 23–27°C, pH 4.0–6.0, native range Upper Orinoco and Negro River basins. FishBase classifies family as Acestrorhamphidae (post-reclassification); hobby sources retain Characidae.

fish.breedingNotes: Requires a dedicated breeding tank set up with very soft, acidic blackwater conditions, dim or blackout lighting, and a slight temperature reduction. Spawning occurs nocturnally and eggs are highly sensitive to light.
